title: Tax creep will eat your ‘WATO’
full_text:
BUDGET 2026 Jim Chalmers will give back about half of what an average worker loses in a year’s worth of bracket creep when he delivers sweeteners in his fifth budget on Tuesday night, where investors could face a further shock of a potential 30 per cent minimum capital gains tax.
The Australian can reveal the Treasurer’s new tax handout – to be called the Working Australian Tax Offset (WATO) and worth about $250 per person for the 2027 financial year, would only clawback part of the $440 in extra tax paid by an average worker due to bracket creep, analysis by The Australian using the Reserve Bank’s inflation forecast shows.
Dr Chalmers has repeatedly said Tuesday’s budget would focus on intergenerational fairness and “rebalancing” the tax treatment on wages and salary versus taxes on assets and capital.
“There is a welcome debate out there about the role of tax reform in trying to rebalance this issue we’ve got between very generous treatment of assets and less generous treatment of labour income of workers,” Dr Chalmers said.
The WATO is at the centre of a budget Labor will claim is a comprehensive reform package that deals with the consequences of the Iran war while improving the chances of young Australians to get ahead and own a home.
But changes in the budget to taxes on capital gains, trusts and negative gearing – as revealed by The Australian – were ruled out by Anthony Albanese and Dr Chalmers at the last election and closely resemble the rejected 2019 election policies of ex-Labor leader Bill Shorten.
As they fight accusations of broken election promises, the Prime Minister and Dr Chalmers are saying decisions in the budget will be made “for the right reasons” in 2026.
The Australian can reveal that, in addition to those changes, the introduction of a minimum 30 per cent tax on all capital gains, with a carve-out for venture capital, has been considered and could be used as part of the government’s plans to remove the 50 per cent discount and replace it with inflation indexation. It was not clear on Monday night if the change had made the final budget papers.
In the past week, the investment community has vented its anger at the changes, arguing such moves could hit investment in housing and other assets and prevent younger people from using the same wealth-creation tools that older people used when they were in their 30s and 40s.
Dr Chalmers has said that the revenue expected from the changes would be small in the first four years.
However economists will be waiting to see if Treasury reveals how much such policies raise in the outer years where the government may be able to promise a return to surplus.
Negative gearing, which allows people to offset their investment property costs against their income, is estimated by the independent Parliamentary Budget Office to be worth $7.9bn in forgone revenue for the federal government in the 2027 financial year. The PBO also estimates that the CGT discount would see $247bn of tax revenue foregone in the next 10 years.
Part of the revenue raised by the government in Tuesday’s budget, including significantly higher tax windfalls from higher commodity prices, would be used to help pay for the WATO.
Applying the RBA’s annual inflation rate forecast of 4.8 per cent, to the bottom two tax thresholds of $18,200 and $45,000 would mean the average worker would be paying $442 in higher taxes for the year. The government’s WATO, would be just over half of that, leaving questions about how serious the government is in tackling bracket creep.
Bracket creep – where inflation pushes workers into higher average tax rates – is estimated by the independent fiscal scorekeeper to push up the government’s personal income tax revenue to 14.5 per cent of GDP by 2035-36, leaving younger people bearing the burden of higher taxes.
“The increasing reliance on personal income tax to balance the budget limits the government’s ability to provide relief from bracket creep, resulting in higher average tax rates for wage earners spreading the tax burden unevenly between generations,” the PBO said in its 2025-26 Medium-Term Budget Outlook: Beyond the Budget.
The PBO specifies that the tax burden will be on the younger generation because its share of income comes more from wages than does that of the older generation. “This shift to increasing the burden on the tax mix to income derived primarily from labour poses equity challenges in the context of Australia’s ageing population,” the PBO says. “Older Australians, who are more likely to derive income from savings, investments, and superannuation, benefit disproportionately from lower taxes, while younger and working-age individuals bear a heavier tax burden on their labour.” Negative gearing, where net rental losses are offset against personal income, is used more by younger people than older people. Australian Taxation Office data shows that about 78 per cent of investors under the age of 40 use negative gearing, significantly higher than the national average of about 49 per cent. The government could provide a clause in the negative gearing change where net rental losses could be used in calculating the cost base for when capital gains taxes are applied, instead of being used to offset personal income.
As interest rates rise negative gearing becomes more appealing. The RBA raised rates for the third time this year last week and warned on government spending which is running at 26.9 per cent of GDP, the highest in 40 years outside the Covid pandemic.
MORE REPORTS P6-
summary: The upcoming 2026 budget by Treasurer Jim Chalmers will introduce the Working Australian Tax Offset (WATO) to partially address bracket creep, while considering a minimum 30% capital gains tax, amid concerns about intergenerational tax fairness.

heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: WATO will provide $250 per person but only partially offsets $440 in extra tax from bracket creep.
A minimum 30% capital gains tax is being considered, potentially impacting investment strategies.
Younger Australians may bear a heavier tax burden due to reliance on personal income tax.
relevance: Understanding these tax changes is crucial for actuaries and financial professionals as they may affect investment strategies and financial planning for clients.
